summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is the purpose of a random sample?

Random sampling is the basis for extending conclusions from a sample to the broader population.

What is the difference between statistical significance and practical significance?

A result can be statistically significant (small p-value) but practically insignificant (trivially small effect), especially with very large samples.

What is the formula sheet most useful for on the AP exam?

Understanding when to use each formula is more important than memorization; the sheet helps with recall during the exam.

What is the most important takeaway from AP Statistics?

AP Statistics teaches critical thinking about data that applies to every field and to everyday decision-making.

A researcher wants to test whether a new fertilizer increases crop yield. She randomly assigns 60 plots to either the new fertilizer (n₁ = 30, mean = 52.3 bushels, s₁ = 6.1) or the standard fertilizer (n₂ = 30, mean = 48.7 bushels, s₂ = 5.8). She performs a two-sample t-test and gets t = 2.33 with p = 0.023. At α = 0.05, what is the correct conclusion?

p = 0.023 < α = 0.05, so we reject H₀. Because this was a randomized experiment, we can conclude the new fertilizer caused higher yields.

A doctor conducts a study on 100 patients with two treatments. She finds p = 0.042 for the two-sample t-test. However, the mean difference is only 0.3 points on a 100-point pain scale. A colleague asks about practical significance. What is the best response?

With large samples, even trivially small effects can be statistically significant. Practical significance considers whether the effect is large enough to matter in the real world.

A regression analysis of study hours (x) vs. exam score (y) yields ŷ = 45 + 4.2x, r = 0.82, r² = 0.6724. A student who studied 10 hours scored 78. What is the residual for this student?

Predicted score = 45 + 4.2(10) = 45 + 42 = 87. Residual = observed − predicted = 78 − 87 = −9.0. The negative residual means the student scored 9 points below the prediction.

A researcher tests whether a coin is fair by flipping it 200 times and observing 115 heads. She uses a one-proportion z-test with H₀: p = 0.5 and Hₐ: p ≠ 0.5. She calculates z = (0.575 − 0.5)/√(0.5 × 0.5/200). What is the z-statistic?

p-hat = 115/200 = 0.575. SE = √(0.5 × 0.5/200) = √(0.25/200) = √0.00125 ≈ 0.03536. z = (0.575 − 0.5)/0.03536 = 0.075/0.03536 ≈ 2.12.

A comprehensive AP Statistics review problem: A factory produces bolts with a target diameter of 10.00 mm. A sample of 40 bolts has mean 10.08 mm and s = 0.15 mm. Test at α = 0.01 whether the mean diameter differs from 10.00 mm. The t-statistic is t = (10.08 − 10.00)/(0.15/√40). What is the t-value and what should the conclusion be, given that the two-tailed critical value for df = 39 at α = 0.01 is approximately ±2.71?

SE = 0.15/√40 = 0.15/6.325 ≈ 0.02372. t = 0.08/0.02372 ≈ 3.37. Since |3.37| > 2.71, we reject H₀ at α = 0.01 and conclude the mean diameter significantly differs from 10.00 mm.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: The four main topics on the AP Statistics exam are:

The AP Statistics framework covers these four broad content areas across the entire course.

Example: On the AP exam, eliminating wrong answers on multiple choice questions:

Process of elimination is a valuable test-taking strategy, especially since there is no penalty for guessing on the AP exam.

Example: What does 'interpret the confidence level' mean on the AP exam?

The confidence level refers to the long-run capture rate of the procedure, not the probability that any single interval contains the parameter.

Example: What is the formula sheet most useful for on the AP exam?

Understanding when to use each formula is more important than memorization; the sheet helps with recall during the exam.

Example: A regression analysis of study hours (x) vs. exam score (y) yields ŷ = 45 + 4.2x, r = 0.82, r² = 0.6724. A student who studied 10 hours scored 78. What is the residual for this student?

Predicted score = 45 + 4.2(10) = 45 + 42 = 87. Residual = observed − predicted = 78 − 87 = −9.0. The negative residual means the student scored 9 points below the prediction.

Example: A school administrator compares SAT math scores between students who took an AP Statistics course and those who did not. AP group (n = 85): mean = 620, s = 75. Non-AP group (n = 120): mean = 580, s = 80. She constructs a 95% CI for the difference in means (AP − Non-AP) and gets (16.2, 63.8). What does this interval tell us?

The interval (16.2, 63.8) does not contain 0, suggesting a significant difference. Since this is observational, we say 'associated with' rather than 'caused.' The CI quantifies the plausible size of the difference.

Example: An AP exam free-response question asks a student to design an experiment to test whether listening to music improves memory recall. Which design earns full credit?

A complete experimental design includes random assignment, a treatment (music), a control (silence), a response variable (recall score), and a statistical analysis plan.
